file_name: Srilatha and Kumar - 2024 - Explainable AI framework for skin cancer classification, and melanoma segmentation.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates an Explainable AI framework for skin cancer classification and melanoma segmentation. It utilizes a modified DenseNet201 deep learning model for classification and advanced convolutional neural network levels for segmentation. The study employs Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanation (LIME) for interpretability and compares performance metrics (IOU, accuracy, precision, recall) against existing models like FPN, MAN, and U-Net.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel 'Explainable AI framework' involving a modified DenseNet201 model for classification and a CNN for segmentation. It details specific methodology (Section III), presents original simulation results with performance metrics (IOU, accuracy, precision, recall) compared against existing models like FPN, MAN, and U-Net, and includes training/validation loss plots. The structure follows a standard research format: Introduction, Literature Survey, Proposed Model, Results, and Conclusion, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: EXPLAINABLE AI FRAMEWORK FOR SKIN CANCER CLASSIFICATION, AND MELANOMA SEGMENTATION
journal: ARPN Journal of Engineering and Applied Sciences
brief_summary: This study proposes an Explainable AI framework for skin cancer classification and melanoma segmentation to address diagnostic challenges in dermatology. The classification phase utilizes a modified DenseNet201 deep learning model, while the segmentation phase employs advanced convolutional neural network levels to delineate melanoma lesion boundaries. To enhance interpretability and clinical trust, the framework integrates Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanation (LIME) for visualizing decision-making processes. The model's performance was evaluated using metrics such as Intersection over Union (IOU), accuracy, precision, and recall, and compared against existing architectures like FPN, MAN, and U-Net. The results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves superior performance and provides accurate, explainable predictions suitable for clinical practice.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on skin cancer classification and specifically melanoma segmentation. The proposed framework uses a modified DenseNet201 for classification and CNN levels for segmentation of melanoma lesion boundaries. While the literature review mentions other classes (e.g., from HAM10000 or ISIC datasets), the proposed model's specific target is melanoma/skin cancer.

The classification task distinguishes between benign and malignant classes. The literature review cites studies (e.g., Nigar et al., Hosny et al.) that classify multiple benign types such as dermatofibroma, squamous cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, melanocytic nevus, vascular lesion, actinic keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma, but the primary focus of the proposed framework is on melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnosis (benign vs malignant classes)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a modified DenseNet201 architecture for skin cancer classification, distinguishing between benign and malignant classes. The model outputs discrete diagnostic categories.

The framework includes a phase for melanoma segmentation using advanced convolutional neural network levels to delineate lesion boundaries in dermoscopic images. Performance is evaluated using Intersection over Union (IOU).

datasets_used.description: International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 dataset, mentioned in the literature survey as a benchmark used by other studies (e.g., reference [10]) for validation.

use.validation: Used for validation in cited literature (reference [10]).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The challenging HAM10000 dataset, mentioned in the literature survey as a benchmark used by other studies (e.g., references [10] and [13]) for assessment.

use.validation: Used for assessment in cited literature (references [10] and [13]).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: International Skin Imaging Collaboration benchmark dataset, mentioned in the literature survey as a benchmark used by other studies (e.g., reference [15]).

use.testing: Used for testing in cited literature (reference [15]).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 dataset, mentioned in the literature survey as the dataset used to validate the XAI model by Nigar et al. [9].

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, IOU, and loss plots.

Proposed Framework (Classification and S.notes: The proposed framework combines a modified DenseNet201 for classification and a CNN for segmentation. The classification results (95.50% accuracy) are attributed to the modified DenseNet201 model in the literature review section describing the authors' prior work or component validation. The segmentation results are explicitly compared against FPN, MANet, and U-Net in Table 2, showing superior performance. The framework utilizes LIME for explainability.

Nigar et al. [9] (Cited Literature).notes: This study validated the XAI model on the ISIC2019 dataset, classifying eight types of skin lesions including dermatofibroma, squamous cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, melanocytic nevus, vascular lesion, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, and innate melanoma.

Naveed Ahmad et al. [10] (Cited Literatu.notes: This study combined two models and used GradCAM-based visualization. It validated the framework using ISIC2018 and HAM10000 datasets.

Research Work [11] (Cited Literature).notes: The classification model accurately showed 96%. The paper reviewed scientific research on CVDL and the explainability of deep learning.

ANFC (Inception-based).metrics: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity (Exact values not reported in text, described as 'better results')

Alwakid et al. [15] (Cited Literature).notes: The proposed approach was tested on the benchmark International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset. The study showed better results in skin cancer identification and classification compared to other approaches.

architecture_used.pretraining: DenseNet201 is pretrained on a relatively large dataset (ImageNet implied by standard DenseNet201 usage, though text states 'pretrained on a relatively large dataset').

classification_model.name: Modified DenseNet201
classification_model.description: Modified architecture using Block A layers for feature extraction, followed by flattening and dense layers for classification. Adjusted specifically for skin lesion classification.

segmentation_model.name: Proposed CNN
segmentation_model.description: Encoder-decoder structure utilizing Conv2D for feature extraction, MaxPooling for downsampling, Conv2DTranspose and UpSampling2D for upsampling/recovery of spatial details, and Dense layers for final output.

optimizers.classification: SGD (Stochastic Gradient Descent) mentioned in context of CNN feature extraction; Adam optimizer mentioned in context of Modified DenseNet201 training phase.

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (Loss plots provided in Figure 11 and 12)

hyperparameters.dropout: Fraction of neuron outputs set to zero during training (specific fraction not reported)
hyperparameters.epochs: Not reported (Training/Validation loss plots shown per epoch)

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation and flipping of input and mask images

classification_or_output_head.classification: Fully connected Dense Layers for binary classification (benign vs malignant)
classification_or_output_head.segmentation: Conv2DTranspose and UpSampling2D layers to recover spatial dimensions and generate segmentation mask

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via rotation and flipping of input and mask images
Hair elimination using the dull razor method (cited from DLCAL-SLDC framework in literature survey)
Noise reduction using average median filter (cited from DLCAL-SLDC framework in literature survey)
Image enhancement using Enhanced Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks (cited from Alwakid et al. in literature survey)
Preprocessing using Top hat filter and inpainting method (cited from Alwakid et al. in literature survey)
Lesion segmentation using GrabCut-based algorithm for ROI isolation (cited from Alwakid et al. and Sikkandar et al. in literature survey)
Tsallis entropy segmentation for lesion isolation (cited from DLCAL-SLDC framework in literature survey)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to input and mask images during the training phase.

conclusions: The proposed Explainable AI framework, combining a modified DenseNet201 for classification and a CNN for segmentation, achieves superior performance compared to existing models like FPN, MANet, and U-Net.
The modified DenseNet201 model achieved a classification accuracy of 95.50%, with a sensitivity of 93.96% and specificity of 97.03%.

The proposed CNN segmentation model achieved an Intersection over Union (IOU) of 94.32% and an accuracy of 94.90% on the test set, outperforming baselines such as FPN (IOU 89.78%), MANet (IOU 89.0%), and U-Net (IOU 83.31%).

The integration of Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanation (LIME) enhances the interpretability of the deep learning models, providing visual explanations that align with clinical expectations and boosting trust among medical practitioners.

The framework is described as smart, easy to use, and capable of providing functional and accurate information, making it suitable for clinical practice in skin cancer diagnosis and melanoma segmentation.

The study acknowledges that challenges in dermatological AI include insufficient/unvarying datasets, limited training sets, class imbalance, and the difficulty of generalizing to unseen conditions, which the proposed explainable approach aims to mitigate.
important_remarks: The paper does not explicitly state the specific dataset used for training and testing the proposed framework; it primarily references ISIC2018, HAM10000, and ISIC2019 in the context of cited literature rather than its own experimental setup.

The classification metrics (95.50% accuracy, 93.96% sensitivity, 97.03% specificity) appear to be derived from a prior study or component validation (Nigar et al. [9] or similar) rather than a new experiment on a clearly defined dataset within this specific paper.

The segmentation performance is compared against baselines (FPN, MANet, U-Net) but lacks details on the specific dataset split (training/validation/testing counts) or demographic diversity of the images used.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is reported to support the claim of superior performance over baseline models.

The study acknowledges general limitations in dermatological AI, such as insufficient/unvarying datasets, limited training sets, class imbalance, and poor generalization to unseen conditions, but does not provide specific mitigation strategies or results for these issues within the proposed framework's evaluation.
The paper claims clinical suitability and trust enhancement via LIME but does not provide any clinical validation, user study with dermatologists, or real-world deployment results.
